title: Sorting
description: Java's built-in Arrays.sort()/Collections.sort() plus manual implementations of bubble sort and selection sort, to understand what's actually happening underneath.
objectives: Use Arrays.sort() and Collections.sort() for everyday sorting
Implement bubble sort and selection sort manually
Compare their O(n^2) complexity against built-in sort's better guarantees

sections.text: Arrays.sort() works on plain arrays. Collections.sort() (recall the Collections Utility Class lesson) does the equivalent for a List. Both are highly optimized -- for primitives, Java's Arrays.sort() uses a dual-pivot quicksort variant, generally O(n log n) on average, far better than the manual algorithms we'll build below.
2. Bubble Sort: Repeatedly Swap Adjacent Out-of-Order Pairs

sections.title: Tracing bubbleSort's first outer pass on {5,2,8,1,9}
steps: After pass 1: the LARGEST element (9) has 'bubbled' to its correct final position at the end

sections.text: Each full pass 'bubbles' the largest remaining unsorted element to its correct position at the end -- that's why the inner loop's upper bound shrinks by i each time (arr.length - 1 - i): the last i elements are already confirmed sorted and don't need rechecking.

Bubble sort is O(n^2) -- nested loops, similar in shape to the Big-O Notation hasDuplicate example. It's taught for understanding, not used in real production code, since Arrays.sort() is dramatically faster for anything beyond tiny inputs.
3. Selection Sort: Repeatedly Find the Minimum

sections.title: Tracing selectionSort's first outer pass on {5,2,8,1,9}
steps: i=0: scan from j=1 to 4 for the smallest value -- minIndex ends at 3 (value 1)
Position 0 is now permanently correct -- next pass (i=1) will find the minimum of the REMAINING unsorted portion

sections.text: Selection sort finds the MINIMUM of the remaining unsorted portion and swaps it into place -- the opposite direction of bubble sort's repeated adjacent swaps, but the same O(n^2) complexity, since it's still a nested loop over the same input.

sections.title: Sorting approaches compared
rows: Use this in real code -- highly optimized, battle-tested
Educational -- repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order pairs
Educational -- repeatedly selects the minimum of the remaining unsorted portion

sections.text: In real-world code, always reach for Arrays.sort() or Collections.sort() (with a custom Comparator when needed, from the Comparable vs Comparator lesson). Manual sorts like bubble and selection sort exist here purely to build intuition for how sorting algorithms actually work -- important groundwork for understanding time complexity trade-offs in general.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Implementing bubble sort or selection sort by hand in real production code, instead of using the far more efficient built-in Arrays.sort()/Collections.sort().
Forgetting to shrink bubble sort's inner loop bound (arr.length - 1 - i) -- without it, already-sorted elements at the end get needlessly rechecked every pass.
Confusing selection sort's approach (find minimum, swap into place) with bubble sort's approach (repeatedly swap adjacent pairs) -- both are O(n^2) but via different mechanics.
Forgetting Arrays.sort() operates on arrays while Collections.sort() operates on Lists -- they aren't interchangeable syntax.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the time complexity of Java's built-in Arrays.sort() for primitive arrays, and how does it compare to bubble sort?
questions.answer: Arrays.sort() for primitives typically runs in O(n log n) on average, using a highly optimized dual-pivot quicksort variant -- significantly faster than bubble sort's O(n^2) for large inputs.

questions.question: How does bubble sort work?
questions.answer: It repeatedly steps through the array, swapping adjacent elements that are out of order. Each full pass 'bubbles' the largest remaining unsorted element to its correct final position at the end.

questions.question: How does selection sort work?
questions.answer: It repeatedly finds the minimum value in the remaining unsorted portion of the array and swaps it into the next correct position -- moving the sorted boundary forward by one element each pass.

questions.question: Why would you use manual sorting algorithms like bubble sort in practice?
questions.answer: Generally you wouldn't in production code -- Arrays.sort()/Collections.sort() are far more efficient. Manual implementations are primarily educational, building intuition for algorithmic thinking and complexity analysis.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Java's Arrays.sort() and Collections.sort() are the practical, real-world tools for sorting -- typically O(n log n) and highly optimized. Bubble sort and selection sort are both O(n^2) educational algorithms: bubble sort repeatedly swaps adjacent out-of-order elements, bubbling the largest remaining value to the end each pass; selection sort repeatedly finds the minimum of the remaining unsorted portion and swaps it into place. Both use nested loops over the same input, giving quadratic complexity, but demonstrate two different strategies for the same underlying goal -- valuable groundwork for reasoning about algorithm design and complexity trade-offs.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: Bubble sort = big bubbles float to the top (end), one swap at a time. Selection sort = repeatedly SELECT the smallest remaining item and place it. Both are O(n^2) -- fine to learn, never to ship; Arrays.sort() ships.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What is the time complexity of bubble sort and selection sort?
How does bubble sort differ from selection sort in approach?
What should you use for sorting in real production code?
Why does bubble sort's inner loop bound shrink with each outer pass?

sections.title: Sort an Array
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers nums, sort the array in ascending order and return it. You must implement a sorting algorithm with a time complexity better than O(n^2).
hints: Since the constraint explicitly forbids O(n^2), bubble/selection sort from this lesson are disqualified -- the problem wants an O(n log n) approach like merge sort.

approaches.label: Brute Force (disqualified by constraints)
approaches.explanation: Selection sort, shown ONLY to illustrate why it fails this problem's explicit O(n log n) requirement.
approaches.output: For nums = [5,2,3,1]: [1,2,3,5] -- correct, but O(n^2), violating the stated constraint

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Merge sort: recursively split the array in half, sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves -- O(n log n), satisfying the constraint.

sections.title: Merge Sorted Array
sections.problemStatement: You are given two integer arrays nums1 and nums2, sorted in non-decreasing order, and two integers m and n, representing the number of elements in nums1 and nums2 respectively. nums1 has a length of m + n, with the last n elements set to 0 (placeholder space). Merge nums2 into nums1 as one sorted array, in place.
hints: Merging from the FRONT risks overwriting nums1 values not yet placed. Merging from the BACK (largest values first) avoids this entirely -- three pointers, all starting at the end.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Copy nums2 into nums1's empty tail space, then sort the whole thing.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Merge from the back: three pointers (end of nums1's real data, end of nums2, end of the combined array), always placing the larger value at the current back position.

sections.title: Sort Colors
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums with n objects colored red, white, or blue (represented as 0, 1, and 2), sort them in place so that objects of the same color are adjacent, in the order red, white, blue -- without using a library sort function.
hints: Since there are only 3 distinct values, this can be solved in ONE pass with three pointers (Dutch National Flag algorithm) -- low, mid, and high -- instead of a general-purpose sort.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count the 0s, 1s, and 2s, then overwrite the array with that many of each in order.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Dutch National Flag algorithm: three pointers partition the array into 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass, without a counting step.

sections.title: Majority Element
sections.problemStatement: Given an array nums of size n, return the majority element -- the element that appears more than n/2 times. You may assume such an element always exists.
hints: If the array is SORTED, the majority element (appearing more than half the time) is guaranteed to occupy the MIDDLE position.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Count occurrences of every distinct value with a HashMap, return whichever exceeds n/2.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort the array -- the majority element, appearing more than half the time, is guaranteed to land at the middle index.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

sections.title: Merge Intervals
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of intervals where intervals[i] = [starti, endi], merge all overlapping intervals, and return an array of the non-overlapping intervals that cover all the intervals in the input.
hints: Sort intervals by START time first -- this is exactly what makes Greedy Algorithms' activity-selection-style scan work: once sorted, only ADJACENT intervals (in sorted order) can possibly overlap.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Repeatedly scan for any pair of overlapping intervals and merge them, until no more merges are possible.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort by start time, then do a single pass -- merge the current interval into the last result interval if they overlap, otherwise start a new one.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting

sections.title: Largest Number
sections.problemStatement: Given a list of non-negative integers nums, arrange them so they form the largest possible number, and return the result as a String.
hints: Standard numeric sorting doesn't apply -- convert each number to a String, then sort with a CUSTOM Comparator (Comparable vs Comparator) comparing (a+b) vs (b+a) as concatenated strings.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: Try every permutation of the numbers (as strings), tracking the lexicographically largest concatenation -- extremely inefficient, shown to illustrate why a smarter comparator is needed.
approaches.output: For nums = [3,30,34,5,9]: plain string sort gives "9534303" -- WRONG (the correct answer is "9534330")
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n), but produces an INCORRECT result for many inputs

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort the number Strings with a custom comparator: a should come before b if (a+b) is lexicographically greater than (b+a).

sections.title: H-Index
sections.problemStatement: Given an array of integers citations where citations[i] is the number of citations a researcher received for their ith paper, return the researcher's h-index -- the maximum h such that the researcher has at least h papers with at least h citations each.

examples.explanation: 3 papers have at least 3 citations each (3, 6, 5), and the remaining papers have no more than 3 citations. So h = 3.

hints: Sort citations in DESCENDING order. Then h is the largest index i (1-based count) where citations[i-1] >= i.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.explanation: For every candidate h from n down to 0, count how many papers have at least h citations, and check if that count is >= h.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.explanation: Sort citations in descending order, then find the largest position i (1-indexed) where citations[i-1] >= i.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the boxed Integer array
